summary:
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the United Nations General Assembly on December 10, 1948, in Paris, with 48 states voting in favor, none against, and eight abstentions (including the Soviet bloc and South Africa). The declaration was drafted by a committee chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt and including representatives from diverse cultural and political traditions, including Lebanese philosopher Charles Malik and Chinese diplomat Peng Chun Chang. It was created in the immediate aftermath of the [[event:112|World War II]] genocides and atrocities, with the explicit intent of establishing universal standards that no state should violate.

The UDHR's thirty articles articulate a comprehensive framework of civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights. It affirms the inherent dignity of all human beings and establishes rights including freedom from torture, arbitrary arrest, and discrimination; freedoms of thought, expression, and religion; the right to a fair trial; and rights to education, work, and participation in cultural life. Though not legally binding as a declaration, it has become the foundational document of international human rights law and has influenced countless national constitutions and international treaties.

The UDHR has been translated into more than 500 languages and remains one of the most translated documents in history. It has been invoked by human rights activists and movements worldwide. Its influence on subsequent international law has been enormous, providing the basis for the two major covenants of 1966 (on civil and political rights, and on economic, social, and cultural rights) and for human rights conventions addressing specific issues including torture, racial discrimination, women's rights, and children's rights.
